title: Why Israel is closely watching the Iran protests

transcript.text: The protests have intensified and we
have a lot of evidence also of very
violent suppression by the Iranian
authorities. Reports of hundreds of
people already killed across Iran. There
there's a real question mark looming
over the future of the of the regime and
there's obviously very being followed
very closely here in Israel as well.
>> What do you mean by that? What uh what
do Israelis or what do the uh Israeli
leadership make of those protests? Well,
the Israeli leadership is very concerned
that this may also spiral out uh to an
attack or even perhaps even another war
between Israel and Iran. We're just 7
months after the 12-day war back in June
between Israel and Iran. And uh in many
ways, this is this is connected to the
protest. Protesters are there because of
the economic situation and the the lack
of democracy in Iran. But a lot of
observers who I've spoken to are saying
that something happened in June and this
may also have in a way broken something
of the barrier of fear that kept that
kept people from from protesting against
the regime.
>> So I'm still a little unclear on what
the Israeli fears are here. Iran has its
own problems at home. What's in it for
them to uh to sort of retaliate now
about what happened in June? So there'll
be a number of reasons why the why the
Iranian regime may want to launch a
strike right now. For for a start, this
perhaps could be something which would
avert the attention or perhaps even
rally the Iranian people or parts of the
Iranian people around the regime.
Another war with Israel. You know, when
you have problems at home, you cast
around for a for an external enemy.
There is also the fear of an Iranian
miscalculation. The Iranians are
themselves very much concerned that
Israel will try again to strike their
country and and to destroy the remaining
missile launchers, the remaining
components of the nuclear program. And
if they fear that that is something that
is about to happen, they want to put in
a preemptive strike first. And then
there's the lingering uh um frustrations
of the Iranian regime from this war that
they didn't succeed in stopping Israel
from attacking their main strategic
sites. They didn't really succeed in in
in landing a serious blow in Israel
despite firing over 500 missiles. And
therefore there's expectation that some
point Iran will try to restore its own
deterrence and restore its military
pride. There's also there's also a worry
in Israel that if the United States, as
Donald Trump has been threatening in
recent days, does attack Iran in some
way. Uh Trump has said that if Iran
continues shooting at protesters, he
will make he will do something very
painful to Iran, then Iranians will have
to retaliate to that. And the easiest
way for them to retaliate would be
hitting one of Americans allies in the
region. These are all reasons why Israel
is concerned that this could actually be
the moment when Iran despite the regime
being very much embattled now at home
may try to take an opportunity and
attack abroad.
>> But what about the prospect of Israel
using this opportunity conversely to to
finish the job it started in June for
example? Well, we saw just two weeks ago
when Netanyahu visited Trump at Mara
Lago that he was basically egging Trump
on to to to support Israel if such a if
such a strike was needed. Trump
delivered on that. He said that I've
heard that the that the Iranians are
rebuilding their their missile and
nuclear programs and if they do, we will
we will take them out again. So there is
that there is American backing for that
and we know that Israel does uh see this
as as something that will have to be
done in the future. But the question
really is the timing and I don't think
from what I've been hearing from Israeli
sources that Israel is quite prepared
now in the in the next days or weeks for
such an attack mainly because Israel is
still replenishing its own missile
defense interceptors. I don't think they
want to do it quite now. So it was
interesting to note the differences
between Netanyahu two weeks ago in Mara
Lago and Netanyao in our interview with
him on Thursday. We interviewed Minimao,
the prime minister of Israel. When I say
we, uh, my boss's editor in chief Sani
Minton Bedos and deputy editor Ed Carr.
I was there as well in the prime
minister's office studio in Jerusalem.
>> Well, exactly that. What did Mr.
Netanyahu's say when when you were sat
down with him last week specifically on
this question about Iran?
>> Well, anyone who's seen who's ever seen
ano interview or speech knows that he's
very very quick to get to Iran and Iran
is, you know, is his main bug bear and
has been for many years. And what was
interesting in our interview with him on
Thursday is that he didn't seem that
eager to speak about Iran. He didn't
bring it up. Uh Zany and Ed brought it
up. And when uh they asked him about uh
Trump's recent statements about Iran,
his threats to attack Iran, if uh if
they do shoot uh at the protesters, he
interestingly he avoided that. He didn't
even endorse that strike. We've had this
strange situation which inal for so many
years has been urging American leaders
to get tough on Iran and now for the
first time we have the president of
United States saying much tougher things
than Netanyahu himself is saying but
beyond that he was he was almost
reluctant to to threaten Iran and he
said well uh revolutions happen best
from within in other words perhaps
America and perhaps other countries
shouldn't be interfering right now we
let things uh work themselves out in
Iran. And obviously he's very hopeful
that there'll be a change that there'll
be regime change. But is he and this is
something that I've spoken with Israeli
officials in recent days that the policy
currently is to lower the Israeli
profile and for it not to see not to be
seen as if Israel is uh in any way
interfering in in the internal issues
within Iran.
>> So that would suggest that Mr. Netanyahu
is trying to head off that
miscalculation you were talking about
before. If uh if Iran feels less
threatened, it is less likely to to
attempt what it thinks is a first
strike.
>> Yes. I mean, it's clear that Netanyahu
hasn't abandoned his plans to uh destroy
Iran's nuclear pro and missile programs.
And he talked about the threat that they
pose. right now he wants this uh um
whatever is happening in Iran if it's
mass protest if it's perhaps even a
regime change and revolution to happen
to play out before there is possibly
another round of warfare between Israel
and Iran
>> so extremely uncertain obviously as as
you say but how do you think Israel
might hope it can turn the situation to
its advantage
>> well there are three uh uh types of
threats and also perhaps gains that
Israel can have here I think the most
immediate concern concern is what we've
mentioned already, the missile, the
ballistic missiles. The nuclear program
currently is dormant, but the missiles
that they've begun to rebuild them. This
is an immediate concern. If for example,
law and order breaks down in in Iran or
parts of Iran go into, you know, descend
into anarchy, then what happens to these
to these very uh dangerous weapons and
and nuclear and this nuclear program?
And this is also something that that the
regime perhaps could use in a deal with
the Americans to let to reduce the
pressure. And we already saw last night
that Donald Trump said that the Iranians
have talked to him about uh about
reaching some kind of a deal. So these
are assets that are in play. I think the
next type of concern is Iran's uh
regional network of proxies which has
been hit very hard by the last couple of
years but it's still there. an Iran
which will have to focus much more now
inwards both on just maintain trying to
maintain order in Iran and on rebuilding
the Iranian economy simply won't have
the money or the attention spent and
continue uh building and and maintaining
this network and then there's a I think
the longer term issue and that is where
Iran fits in in the region. Now, if you
go back before the Iranian revolution in
Iran was Israel's probably most
important ally in the region. Two uh
regional powers, non-Arab, the Iranians
aren't Arabs obviously, and they had a
very had a very good relationship with
Israel. I Israel bought bought most of
its oil from Iran up until the end of
the 1970s. Iran bought a lot of weapons
and other types of technological
knowhow from Israel. All that ended in
1979 when Humeni came to power uh with
his with his Islamic Republic. But you
know you speak to a lot of people who
were around in those days. They don't
see a reason why that kind of alliance
can't exist again. Now this is not
something that's going to happen in days
or weeks even if the regime does fall.
But I think it is a long-term uh
eventuality that we can yeah we can look
towards as certainly as as the desired
result for is for Israel.
>> Thanks very much for your time.
>> Thanks ever for having me Jason.
